claim: Taking a multiple-choice test led to predictions about future performance that were 10% higher than those taking a short-answer test.
note: Phase 1 hypothesis explicitly predicts multiple-choice leads to higher (over-estimated) performance predictions than short-answer -- legitimate pre-specified direction, updated from two_sided to positive. Wave 2: between-subjects factor (F(1,119), numerator df=1), candidate for a t-equivalent Bayes factor once reviewed. [Directionality policy: the published significance test for this result is two-sided, so the Bayes factor is computed two-sided to match. The directional Phase 1 hypothesis is recorded but does not change the sidedness of the comparison; only study_20, whose authors ran a genuinely one-sided test, stays one-sided.]
model_formula: SPSS GLM PercentRember PercentCorrectMC PercentCorrectOE BY Condition /WSFACTOR=factor1 3 Polynomial /WSDESIGN=factor1 /DESIGN=Condition
contrast_direction: between-subjects main effect of testing condition on average predicted future performance
extraction_note: Recomputed from the author's SPSS mixed GLM using listwise-complete cases on Condition, PercentRember, PercentCorrectMC, PercentCorrectOE. Within-subject levels are ordered as in the SPSS syntax: PercentRember, PercentCorrectMC, PercentCorrectOE. n_total = 121; Condition levels = 1; 2; group counts = 1:63; 2:58. Row 35 extracts the between-subjects Condition effect from Tests of Between-Subjects Effects.
